pattern: The sum of the factorials of a number's digits never changes no matter how those digits are shuffled, so instead of generating every permutation and testing each one, compute that fixed sum once and check whether ITS digits are simply a rearrangement of the original digits.
hints: Rearranging the digits of n never changes which digits it has, and therefore never changes the sum of their factorials — that sum is one fixed number, computable once from n directly.
Precompute factorials of 0 through 9. Add up the factorial of every digit of n to get a single candidate value S; this S is the only number any permutation of n's digits could possibly equal.

n has a permutation that is digitorial exactly when S is made of the very same digits (same multiset, same counts) as n — compare the sorted digit list of n against the sorted digit list of S; no leading-zero permutation needs to be constructed explicitly, since a valid rearrangement exists whenever the multisets match.

edge_inputs.description: 10 — no permutation of its digits can be digitorial
single digit 1 — trivially digitorial (0!/1! style fixed point)
single digit 9 — not a fixed point (9! != 9)
514 is NOT itself digitorial, but a permutation of its digits (145) is — must check permutations, not just n as-is
40585 is a larger known digitorial number with a repeated digit
100 has trailing zeros; the factorial sum's digit multiset does not match {1,0,0}
999999999 — largest repeated-digit number just under the ceiling

stress.description: random n up to 999999, seeded — kept below the full 10-digit range so the permutation-based oracle stays fast

complexity.time: O(d log d) where d is the number of digits of n
